title: SEGMENT 16 — Antibody fragments and nanobodies — when smaller is better

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Antibody fragments and nanobodies — when smaller is better
Until now, every antibody we've discussed has been a full Y-shaped molecule — two heavy chains, two light chains, both Fab arms, full Fc region.
That's a big molecule — about 150 kilodaltons, which by protein standards is hefty.
For many applications, that size is fine.
But sometimes, bigger is worse, and you want a smaller version.
Why might you want a smaller antibody?
Reason one: tissue penetration.
Big molecules don't diffuse into solid tumors very efficiently — they get stuck at the outer layers and can't reach the deep cancer cells in the core.
A smaller molecule can penetrate deeper.
Reason two: clearance.
Big molecules with an intact Fc hang around in the bloodstream for weeks; sometimes you want a molecule that clears fast — for imaging, or where prolonged exposure isn't desirable.
Reason three: access to hidden epitopes.
Some targets have binding sites that are tucked away in clefts that a full antibody's bulky structure simply cannot reach into.
A smaller molecule can fit.
Reason four: production cost.
Smaller proteins are easier and cheaper to make.
So scientists have spent decades engineering minimal versions of antibodies that retain the binding specificity but shed the bulk.
The simplest fragment is the Fab itself — just one arm of the Y, no Fc, no second arm.
About one-third the size of a full antibody.
Already smaller, already cleared faster, no Fc-mediated effector functions.
Useful when you want pure binding without recruitment of immune cells.
Going smaller, you can make a single-chain Fv, or scFv.
This is just the variable domains of the heavy and light chains, glued together with a short flexible peptide linker.
About one-sixth the size of a full antibody.
Just the binding part.

ScFvs are the building blocks of many engineered constructs — they're often the recognition modules inside bispecifics and inside the CAR molecules of CAR-T therapy, which we'll get to in the last segment.
But the most fascinating small antibody variant comes from a quirky discovery in camels and llamas.

In 1989, a group of Belgian researchers noticed that the serum of camels — and, it later turned out, all camelids, including llamas and alpacas — contains antibodies that are unusual: they have only heavy chains, no light chains at all.

The Fab arm of these camelid antibodies is therefore built from heavy chain alone, and the variable domain at the tip is a single small unit, about one-tenth the size of a normal antibody, that binds antigen all by itself.
These single-domain antibody fragments have been named nanobodies — for their small size, nanometers across, rather than for any nanotechnology connection.

They're extraordinarily small, extraordinarily stable, often refoldable after heating, can bind into clefts that conventional antibodies can't reach — and they're discovered by immunizing a llama and harvesting its B cells.
(Yes, real working pharmaceutical labs maintain llama herds for this purpose.)
An entire industry has grown up around nanobody technology, and the first nanobody drug, caplacizumab, was approved in 2019 for a rare bleeding disorder.
So when you hear "nanobody," think: tiny, stable, llama-derived, binding-only, no Fc, no effector functions, but extraordinarily versatile as a building block.
You can chain them together — fuse two or three nanobodies into a single small molecule that hits multiple targets at once.
You can attach them to imaging tags, to small molecules, to anything you want.
The minimalism is the feature.
The general principle: the full Y-shape is overkill for many applications, and the antibody field has spent decades finding ways to chop the Y down to just the parts you actually need.
Fab, scFv, nanobody — three steps down a ladder of size reduction, each with its own use cases.
